title: Cat Swallowed String: Emergency Triage & Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat swallowed string, yarn, or ribbon, it's a high-priority emergency. Learn the risks of linear foreign bodies and why you must see a vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ingesting a string or ribbon is a high-priority medical emergency that can cause life-threatening intestinal damage. Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately and take photos of any remaining material to assist with triage.

What is a linear foreign body and why is it dangerous for my cat?

When a cat ingests string, thread, dental floss, or ribbon, it is classified as a linear foreign body. Unlike a solid object, string-like materials can become anchored at the base of the tongue or in the stomach. As the intestines attempt to push the rest of the string through the digestive tract, the bowel segments bunch up like a drawstring, which can lead to severe damage or life-threatening blockages.

Is it an emergency if my cat swallowed a string?

  • Urgency Level: High. Ingestion of string is a critical medical emergency that requires immediate attention.
  • Internal damage can progress rapidly even if your cat appears healthy immediately after ingestion.
  • If the string becomes taut, it can saw through the intestinal walls, causing perforation and life-threatening abdominal infection (peritonitis).
  • You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately for professional guidance.

How can providing a photo help the veterinarian triage my cat?

  • If you have the remaining portion of the string, take a photo next to a common object like a coin or ruler to show the texture and estimated length swallowed.
  • If you see string under the cat's tongue or protruding from their rear, take a clear photo to help the vet assess the situation safely.
  • Photos allow the veterinary team to prepare for your arrival and assess the severity without causing the cat additional stress or pain.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of suspected foreign body ingestion in cats, particularly linear foreign bodies such as thread, yarn, or dental floss which may be difficult to palpate, physical examination must include a thorough inspection of the oral cavity for anchoring. If a linear foreign body is present in the oral cavity, it must be cut immediately and never pulled. Clinical signs can vary but often include vomiting and anorexia. Vomiting is less common with distal small-intestinal obstruction. Signs of hypovolemic shock and abdominal pain usually accompany intestinal incarceration. Laboratory findings may reveal leukocytosis with a mild left shift, or marked leukocytosis or leukopenia with a degenerative left shift in cases of GI perforation and secondary bacterial peritonitis.

Chapter: Surgery, Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 392)

faqs.question: Can I pull the string out if I see it under the tongue or tail?
faqs.answer: No. Never pull the string. If the string is anchored further down the digestive tract, pulling it can cause the material to slice through the esophagus, stomach, or intestines, leading to fatal internal injuries.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of a blockage?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include repetitive vomiting, loss of appetite, hiding, abdominal pain, and lethargy. However, do not wait for these symptoms to appear before seeking veterinary care.

faqs.question: How will the veterinarian treat my cat?
faqs.answer: The vet will perform a physical exam and likely use X-rays or an ultrasound to check for intestinal bunching. Treatment may involve induced vomiting (only if safe), endoscopic removal, or emergency surgery to remove the obstruction.
